description.en: Go beyond K-Means with density-based clustering (DBSCAN), hierarchical agglomerative clustering, and Gaussian Mixture Models for soft assignments.
description.ta: K-Means-ஐ தாண்டி அடர்த்தி அடிப்படையிலான கிளஸ்டரிங் (DBSCAN), படிநிலை திரட்டு கிளஸ்டரிங், மற்றும் மென்மையான ஒதுக்கீடுகளுக்கான காஸியன் கலவை மாடல்களை கற்றுக்கொள்ளுங்கள்.

story_variants.en:
## Vaathiyaar's Three Strategies

Vaathiyaar reveals three different sorting tables. "K-Means assumes round groups. But what if your groups are crescent-shaped? Or nested? Or you don't even know how many groups there are?"

### DBSCAN (Density-Based)
- No need to specify K!
- Finds clusters of arbitrary shape
- Labels outliers as noise (-1)
- Key params: eps (neighborhood radius), min_samples

### Hierarchical Clustering
- Builds a tree (dendrogram) of merges
- Cut the tree at any height to get different numbers of clusters
- Linkage methods: ward, complete, average, single

### Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)
- Soft clustering: each point gets a probability of belonging to each cluster
- Can model elliptical clusters (unlike K-Means which assumes spherical)

```python
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN, AgglomerativeClustering
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture
from sklearn.datasets import make_moons
import numpy as np

# Non-spherical data
X, y = make_moons(n_samples=300, noise=0.1, random_state=42)

# DBSCAN
db = DBSCAN(eps=0.2, min_samples=5)
db_labels = db.fit_predict(X)
print(f'DBSCAN clusters: {len(set(db_labels)) - (1 if -1 in db_labels else 0)}')
print(f'Noise points: {(db_labels == -1).sum()}')

# Hierarchical
agg = AgglomerativeClustering(n_clusters=2, linkage='ward')
agg_labels = agg.fit_predict(X)

# GMM
gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=2, random_state=42)
gmm_labels = gmm.fit_predict(X)
print(f'GMM probabilities (first 3): {gmm.predict_proba(X[:3]).round(3)}')
```

models.name: DBSCAN
models.strengths: Any shape, finds outliers, no K needed

models.name: Hierarchical
models.strengths: Dendrogram visualization, no K needed upfront

explanation.en: Import density-based and hierarchical clustering algorithms
Import GMM for probabilistic soft clustering
Generate crescent-shaped data — K-Means fails on this shape
300 points in two interlocking crescents — a classic non-linear clustering test
eps: maximum distance between neighbors; min_samples: minimum points to form a cluster
Fit and assign clusters in one step — label -1 means noise/outlier

steps.line: n_clusters = len(set(db_labels)) - (1 if -1 in db_labels else 0)

explanation.en: DBSCAN finds 2 crescents and identifies noise points automatically
Ward linkage minimizes variance when merging clusters — most common choice
Hierarchical clustering: starts with each point as its own cluster, merges bottom-up
GMM with 2 Gaussian components — each models one cluster
Fit: learn the mean, covariance, and weight of each Gaussian component
Soft assignment: each point gets a probability for each cluster
BIC helps choose the optimal number of components — lower is better

animation_sequence.code:
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN, AgglomerativeClustering
from sklearn.datasets import make_moons
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

X, _ = make_moons(n_samples=200, noise=0.1, random_state=42)

db = DBSCAN(eps=0.2, min_samples=5)
db_labels = db.fit_predict(X)
n_clusters = len(set(db_labels)) - (1 if -1 in db_labels else 0)
print(f'DBSCAN: {n_clusters} clusters, {(db_labels==-1).sum()} noise points')

agg = AgglomerativeClustering(n_clusters=2, linkage='ward')
agg_labels = agg.fit_predict(X)
print(f'Agglomerative silhouette: {silhouette_score(X, agg_labels):.3f}')

executionSteps.explanation: Moon-shaped clusters — KMeans would fail here
DBSCAN: density-based, no need to specify k
Points in dense regions = cluster, sparse = noise (-1)

instruction.en: Generate make_moons data. Apply KMeans(2), DBSCAN(eps=0.3), and GaussianMixture(2). Compare which correctly identifies the two moon shapes by printing the cluster assignments for the first 5 points.
instruction.ta: make_moons தரவை உருவாக்குங்கள். KMeans(2), DBSCAN(eps=0.3), மற்றும் GaussianMixture(2) பயன்படுத்துங்கள். முதல் 5 புள்ளிகளுக்கான கிளஸ்டர் ஒதுக்கீடுகளை அச்சிட்டு ஒப்பிடுங்கள்.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans, DBSCAN
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture
from sklearn.datasets import make_moons
from sklearn.metrics import adjusted_rand_score

X, y_true = make_moons(n_samples=300, noise=0.1, random_state=42)

# K-Means
km_labels = KMeans(n_clusters=2, random_state=42, n_init=10).fit_predict(X)

# DBSCAN
db_labels = DBSCAN(eps=___, min_samples=5).fit_predict(X)

# GMM
gmm_labels = GaussianMixture(n_components=___, random_state=42).fit_predict(X)

for name, labels in [('KMeans', km_labels), ('DBSCAN', db_labels), ('GMM', gmm_labels)]:
    ari = adjusted_rand_score(y_true, labels)
    print(f'{name:12s}: ARI={ari:.3f}')
practice_challenges.expected_output:
KMeans      : ARI=0.456
DBSCAN      : ARI=0.952
GMM         : ARI=0.456

en: eps=0.3 works well for the moons data
DBSCAN should have the highest ARI because moons are non-spherical
ta: moons தரவுக்கு eps=0.3 நன்றாக வேலை செய்கிறது
DBSCAN அதிகபட்ச ARI கொண்டிருக்க வேண்டும் ஏனெனில் moons கோள வடிவமற்றவை

q.en: What is the key advantage of GMM over K-Means?
q.ta: K-Means-ஐ விட GMM-ன் முக்கிய நன்மை என்ன?

en: It always finds the correct K

q.ta: எப்போது DBSCAN K-Means-ஐ விட சிறப்பாக செயல்படும்?

en: When clusters have arbitrary shapes and there are outliers
When you know the exact number of clusters
ta: கிளஸ்டர்கள் தன்னிச்சையான வடிவங்கள் கொண்டிருக்கும்போது மற்றும் வெளிப்புற மதிப்புகள் இருக்கும்போது
